title: Baseline Forecasts: The Naive Benchmark
concept: Always benchmark against simple guesses: 'tomorrow will equal today' (naive) or 'tomorrow will equal same day last week' (seasonal naive).

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main characteristic of walk-forward validation (Time Series Split)?

options.text: The training data always precedes the test data chronologically as the cutoff rolls forward.
It shuffles the training and test sets to ensure variety.
It uses only future data to predict past data.

exercises.explain: Correct! Walk-forward validation rolls forward through time, keeping training data chronologically before test data.

cards.title: The weather guesser
cards.body: If you want to show that your fancy AI weather model is smart, you must beat the simplest guess: "Tomorrow's weather will be the same as today." If your complex model can't beat that simple guess, it's not very useful!

cards.title: Naive vs. Seasonal Naive
cards.body: A baseline forecast is a simple, no-effort guess used as a benchmark. The naive forecast assumes the next value will equal the most recent value (e.g., tomorrow's sales = today's sales). The seasonal naive forecast assumes the value will equal the same period in the previous cycle (e.g., this Sunday's sales = last Sunday's sales).

cards.title: Racing against a turtle
cards.body: If you build a sports car, you don't brag about beating a walking turtle. But if your sports car loses to the turtle, you know something is seriously broken! The naive forecast is the turtle — it's the minimum standard your AI model must outrun.

cards.title: Weekly restaurant sales
cards.body:
A restaurant forecasts burger sales:
• Naive guess: "We sold 100 burgers on Saturday, so we will sell 100 on Sunday."
• Seasonal naive guess: "We sold 150 burgers last Sunday, so we will sell 150 this Sunday."
A smart model should easily beat both by looking at weekly cycles.

cards.title: Simple baselines are not useless
cards.body:
Myth: Naive forecasts are just silly guesses that are never used in real business.
Reality: In highly volatile markets (like stocks or rapid weather shifts), the naive forecast is actually very hard to beat! It serves as a sanity check. If your model is worse than the naive benchmark, it is likely overfitting.

cards.title: Naive vs. Seasonal Naive
cards.body: How simple baseline forecasts predict the future:

left.text:
Predicts the last observed value.
Formula: Tomorrow = Today.
Best for stable, slow-changing data.

right.text:
Predicts the value from the last cycle.
Formula: Next Monday = Last Monday.
Best for strong periodic patterns.

exercises.prompt: What does a naive forecast baseline do?

options.text: It predicts that the next value will be identical to the most recent value.
It uses decision trees to calculate the average value.
It randomly shuffles history to find a baseline.
It automatically detects anomalies in the timeline.

exercises.explain: Exactly! A naive forecast baseline simply sets the next prediction to the most recent actual observation.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a complex forecasting model has a higher average error than a seasonal naive benchmark, it is ready to be deployed.
exercises.explain: False. If a complex model cannot outperform a simple baseline, it is not ready for deployment and needs adjustment.

exercises.sentence: A seasonal naive forecast predicts that this Friday's sales will equal ___ Friday's sales.
exercises.explain: A seasonal naive forecast uses the value from the exact same point in the previous cycle, which would be last Friday.

exercises.prompt: Which prediction represents a naive forecast baseline?

options.text: Since we sold 50 cups of coffee today, we predict we will sell 50 cups tomorrow.
Since we sold 50 cups today and 40 yesterday, we predict we will sell 45 tomorrow.
Based on our trend analysis, coffee sales will rise by 12% tomorrow.

exercises.explain: Predicting tomorrow's sales will be identical to today's (50 cups) is the definition of a naive forecast.

exercises.prompt: You build a model to forecast daily electricity usage. A seasonal naive baseline (predicting today's usage equals last week's same-day usage) has an average error of 10 kilowatts. Your ARIMA model has an average error of 12 kilowatts. What should you do?

options.text: Do not deploy the ARIMA model yet; it is underperforming the simple baseline, which means it is either overfitting or needs better features.
Deploy the ARIMA model immediately because it is more complex and therefore better.
Delete the baseline and report that the ARIMA model is 100% accurate.
Convert the electricity data into a classification problem to hide the error.

exercises.explain: Correct! If a complex model cannot beat a simple benchmark, it should not be deployed. You must improve the model or stick with the simpler baseline.
